data.title: Executive of Hong Kong’s Apple Daily ‘regrets’ not standing up to ex-boss Jimmy Lai
data.description:
The lawyer representing a former Apple Daily senior executive has said his client regretted not standing firmly by her journalistic principles against the editorial interference of her ex-boss Jimmy Lai Chee-ying.
Three High Court judges adjourned sentencing Lai and eight others until an unspecified date after defence lawyers wrapped up their mitigation arguments at West Kowloon Court on Tuesday.
Appeals for leniency were made on behalf of six former senior executives of Apple Daily, who pleaded guilty before Lai’s conviction for conspiracies to collude with foreign forces and to print seditious articles.
Publisher Cheung Kim-hung, associate publisher Chan Pui-man, editor-in-chief Ryan Law Wai-kwong, executive editor-in-chief Lam Man-chung, managing editor Fung Wai-kong and editorial writer Yeung Ching-kee admitted using the tabloid-style newspaper to instigate hatred against authorities and attract international sanctions before its closure in June 2021.
Chan’s lawyer, Marco Li Kwok-wai, said her professionalism had been held in high regard, with the defendant previously receiving journalism awards from the Society of Publishers in Asia and the Chinese University of Hong Kong for her coverage of international events.
He highlighted comments by May Chan Suk-mei, a news director at Commercial Radio, who praised the defendant for her professional work ethic.
Li said his client had tried convincing Lai not to use Apple Daily to appeal to US President Donald Trump to intervene in Hong Kong affairs in May 2020, but the former media boss had gone ahead with his plan.
In October 2020, Chan opposed Lai’s idea for a political satire on Apple Daily’s digital platforms - a programme similar to the now-suspended RTHK series Headliner - but the newspaper founder insisted on his proposal, the lawyer said.
Li said Chan had not been able to resign due to financial pressure and an emotional attachment to her job, adding that she was currently suffering from “serious health issues”.
“She now very much regrets that she didn’t uphold her professionalism more firmly against the matters that she’s not comfortable with,” he said.
Lam’s counsel, Erik Shum Sze-man, said his client had played a “very limited” role in the scheme because he was only responsible for Apple Daily’s printed news stories but not its commentaries and online content.
“I hope the court will look at Mr Lam as a person of impeccable character, but for this case, he was dragged into muddy waters as an employee, and he had no decisive role in anything that indicated he was heavily involved in this conspiracy,” Shum said.

A retired High Court judge who is a personal friend of Lam’s had written a mitigation letter on his behalf, the court heard. The Post understands the former judge is Patrick Li Hon-leung.
Shum also argued that the national security law did not expressly define the gravity of instigating sanctions against mainland China because the offence was drafted by “someone we don’t know”, a reference to the country’s central authorities.
Mr Justice Alex Lee Wan-tang said the implication of that contention was “a little bit too bold”, but stopped short of criticising the lawyer.
Madam Justice Esther Toh Lye-ping said the court would inform parties in writing about the sentencing date as soon as practicable. Lee added that the bench would need to tackle “important questions of law” in the ruling.
Collusion with foreign forces carries a two-tier sentencing regime, with a minimum jail term of 10 years and up to life imprisonment applicable to a case “of a grave nature”.
Lee said on Monday it was “unrealistic” to contend that the present offence was minor enough to warrant a jail term of between three to 10 years under the second tier.
The court has heard oral submissions from counsel representing Lai and three others in a previous hearing.

data.title: Hong Kong artist Jaffa Lam’s ceramic forest inspired by old Shanghai staircases
data.description:
One of the most striking installations in the ongoing 15th edition of the Shanghai Biennale is Hong Kong mixed-media artist Jaffa Lam Laam’s forest of ceramic columns, called Windbreak (2025).
The columns are similarly shaped, but their sizes vary from as small as a flute to four metres (13 feet) high, large enough to support a room. The vitrified green and grey glaze is partial and uneven, leaving the oxidised, reddish-brown clay exposed in many areas. This creates a beautiful contrast between the matt and rough surfaces.
Lam is best known for site-specific, large-scale works that she often creates using found, recycled or repurposed materials. Ceramics is a relatively new medium for her.
“I wanted to study ceramics at university, but it never really worked out,” she says. “So I set it aside until I went on an art residency in Longquan, [in China’s] Zhejiang province, in 2024.”

Lam, who was born in Fujian province in 1973 and moved to Hong Kong when she was 12, has always been attentive to the social and historical value of skills in fading industries, from textile factories to oyster farming.
In 2025, for her solo exhibition in Shenzhen’s Shekou area, called “Behind the Blue”, she used discarded blue plastic oyster barrels from the oyster farms between Shenzhen and Hong Kong to create a work that contrasts the freedom enjoyed by nature with artificial boundaries set by humans.
During Art Basel Hong Kong in 2023, she produced a monumental piece called Trolley Party with recycled umbrella fabric and industrial trolleys, expanding on the themes of labour and collective identity that she has long explored, often collaborating with female factory workers for whom she holds workshops.
With Windbreak, the original inspiration was not the jade-like green celadon stoneware that Longquan has been making since at least the Jin dynasty (266-420). Instead, it was a more modest material: discarded wooden stair-rail posts from old Shanghai houses being demolished.
The shapes, details and irregularity of the wooden pieces intrigued her. “You don’t notice it at first, but every piece of wood is turned differently,” she says.

In Shanghai, these remnants of the past are now considered antiques, Lam adds. But in Longquan, a city where crafts beyond ceramics are still very much alive, she found entire streets of skilled woodworkers.
“It is a very old city, and they have a lot of old, traditional wooden buildings, and craftsmen who know how to renovate them,” she says.
As the Shanghai Biennale, themed “Does the flower hear the bee?”, is centred on nature and the climate crisis the world faces, Lam felt the wooden stair-rail posts – once standing like soldiers or guardian deities in old houses, now discarded and replaced – were an apt metaphor for the way “nature tries to protect humans, even if people do not really treasure that contribution”. Hence the name of Lam’s installation, Windbreak, meaning a row of trees or shrubs that protect farms from strong winds.
Seen as a symbol of broader society, the posts also evoke both the importance and the vulnerability of the memories that help define a home and community.
“Those staircases connected old Shanghai,” Lam says. Often, the houses have been converted into both legal and illegal subdivided flats. The wooden staircases would be a link between different social classes – the wealthy original owners and the modern inhabitants – as well as the past and the present.

Transposing the shapes into ceramics not only fuses primordial materials such as wood, soil and fire to awaken awareness of the bounty of nature, but also pays tribute to the people who have kept ancient cultural legacies alive.
Each ceramic column is unique. Lam regards each piece as an individual, with some standing upright and some leaning slightly backward, as if being pushed by an invisible force.
She had to overcome technical challenges. “I wanted to do them all different, and on a much larger scale, which meant a lot of trial and error,” she says. “It was a learning process. I spent five months on this one project, mixing different local clays and glazes.”
Now, she is working on a new solo exhibition at Axel Vervoordt Gallery, in Wong Chuk Hang, opening in March during Art Basel Hong Kong.
Shifting from the grand scale of Windbreak, she is turning to smaller, more intimate works that place greater emphasis on her personal history and emotions, while continuing to explore her long-standing themes of community, heritage and the transformation of everyday objects into art.
“I am a very site-specific and city-oriented person,” she says, “but most of my work is about people.”
Jaffa Lam’s solo exhibition will be at Axel Vervoordt Hong Kong from March 21 to May 23.

data.title: Thailand election: the cash splash pledges aiming to win votes
data.description:
Thailand’s election season has triggered a dizzying array of cash giveaway offers from parties vying for votes, from a US$2,000 payment for newborns to debt write-offs and US$100 monthly top-ups for the poorest households to combat pay worth US$6,300.
But economists warn that in a slowing economy saddled with debt amid political gridlock, such lavish pledges are highly unlikely to revive growth.
Many Thais, like 34-year-old Ton, see the handouts as a distraction from the deeper problems afflicting the Thai economy.
“My salary has not changed in years, but everything else has gone up in price,” said the hotel worker in Bangkok who earns 18,000 baht (US$580) a month and gave only one name.
“I am getting poorer and there is nothing any party can do without fixing the entire economy.”

Ton’s frustrations reflect a broader malaise that could shape how millions vote in the February 8 poll. Household debt hovers around 90 per cent of gross domestic product and the economy grew just 2 per cent last year – the slowest among Southeast Asia’s major economies – with forecasts pointing even lower in 2026.
Once Asia’s star economic performer, Thailand now risks being overtaken by Vietnam within a decade, according to forecasters at the Centre for Economics and Business Research.
The country has cycled through three prime ministers since the last election in 2023, leaving little space for coherent policymaking.
But the appetite for big-ticket promises, emblazoned on election posters across the country, remains undimmed.

Bidding for votes
The Democrat Party is dangling payments of 65,000 baht (US$2,080) for new parents in a bid to drive up the country’s birth rate as it rapidly ages. Thailand’s oldest party, led by former prime minister Abhisit Vejjajiva, is touting clean government and sensible spending as a vote winner.
His conservative, royalist party has little chance of winning the election, but could yet play a role in deciding who ultimately governs.
The United Thai Nation Party, born of the 2014 coup and backed by army generals, is offering hefty incentives for military service – 30,000 baht for voluntary enlistment and 200,000 baht for combat duty – after last year’s deadly clashes with Cambodia raised fears of a broader border war.

Pheu Thai, meanwhile, is returning to the populist pledges that saw it sweep Thailand’s elections in the first decades of this century, albeit under a variety of names. Thaksin Shinawatra’s political vehicle, now under the leadership of his nephew Yodchanan Wongsawat, promises expansive debt forgiveness, 20-baht daily public transport passes in Bangkok and cash top-ups to ensure Thailand’s poorest earn at least 3,000 baht per month.
Yodchanan has also proposed a 10-year plan coupling digital innovation with his party’s war on poverty, pitching technology investment as the key to a productivity revival in a televised interview on Monday.

But voters are unlikely to have forgotten that Pheu Thai’s last campaign pledge – a 10,000-baht digital cash handout – was only partly delivered, while its flagship casino tourism plan collapsed amid coalition infighting.
Then there is the People’s Party, which won the popular vote in 2023 under a previous incarnation before being sidelined by the conservative establishment.
Nothing less than Thailand’s reputation as Asia’s “fifth tiger” is on the line, according to Veerayuth Kanchoochat, one of the party’s candidates for prime minister.
“If the Thai economy does not recover within the next five years, the ‘fifth tiger of Asia’ will permanently become nothing more than local folklore,” he said on Sunday, referring to the four “Asian tiger” economies of Hong Kong, Singapore, Taiwan and South Korea that became renowned for their rapid industrialisation and export-led growth from the 1960s to the 1990s.

His party has pledged to curb the influx of cheap Chinese imports, tighten control over the grey economy and push manufacturing up the value chain into high-value sectors such as semiconductors.
Trailling just behind in opinion polls is Bhumjaithai, led by caretaker Prime Minister Anutin Charnvirakul, who has shrewdly positioned himself as a stabilising force.
His party is eschewing cash handouts, instead promising policies aimed at older voters: life insurance, cancer care guarantees, job creation schemes and targeted grants.
“Our focus is not on giving away fish,” he said in a television appearance on Monday. “We focus on giving Thai people the fishing hooks.”

Whoever wins, economists say Thailand needs more stability – rather than cash giveaways – to escape stagnation.
“The faster we can establish political stability, the better for the policy implementation and overall confidence,” said Pimnara Hirankasi, chief economist at Krungsri, Thailand’s fifth largest bank.
“But in the long term, what really matters is whether the new government is willing and able to tackle our structural issues … these are deep-rooted problems that require serious policy commitment.”

data.title: ‘Resolution revolution’ pioneer Alexey Amunts leaves Max Planck to join China’s SMART
data.description:
Alexey Amunts, whose pioneering work on mitochondrial ribosomes helped catalyse the cryo-electron microscopy “resolution revolution”, has left the Max Planck Institute in Germany to join China’s Shenzhen Medical Academy of Research and Translation (SMART) this month.
Amunts confirmed he had joined SMART as a senior investigator and would “contribute to its international relations agenda”.
He is internationally recognised for his breakthroughs in visualising life’s molecular machinery at near-atomic resolution. Britain’s Journal of Cell Science featured him as a “cell scientist to watch” in 2021.


Amunts’ relocation highlights China’s growing appeal as a hub for cutting-edge basic science and translational innovation.
“Shenzhen adds another dimension; it is a place where ideas do not remain ideas for long. The density of talent, technology and manufacturing capacity makes it unusually practical to move from concept to implementation,” Amunts said.
“I chose SMART because it combines long-term scientific ambition with a system that can execute quickly and connect excellent basic research to translational impact internationally,” he added.
SMART announced his appointment on social media on January 7. Its head, Yan Ning, commented on and shared her post to welcome him to the academy’s Institute of Bio-Architecture and Bio-Interactions (IBABI).
Russian-born Amunts, who told the South China Morning Post his new position and foreign work permit application were expected to take a few weeks to finalise, reposted the news to his Weibo account, writing, “We’re forming a lab at SMART. Come to SZ to build your next chapter with us”.
Before joining the Shenzhen institution, he was a Humboldt Scholar at the Max Planck Institute in Germany and was a tenured professor at Stockholm University.
He received his PhD from Tel Aviv University in 2010. He then conducted postdoctoral research at the MRC Laboratory of Molecular Biology in Cambridge, where he studied the cryo-electron microscopy (cryo-EM) structure of mitochondrial ribosomes, or mitoribosomes.
His work on the mitoribosome contributed significantly to the “resolution revolution” in structural biology. The term “resolution revolution” is widely used to describe the transformative impact of cryo-EM, highlighted by breakthroughs such as the first high-resolution structure of the mitoribosome reported in journals like Science.
Mitochondria are organelles that act as cellular powerhouses. They use inhaled oxygen and nutrients from food to produce the energy essential for life. This process is carried out by intricate molecular machines embedded in the inner mitochondrial membrane.
Cryo-EM has enabled scientists to determine the high-resolution structures of these large protein complexes and other cellular machinery.
In 2016, Amunts established his own independent laboratory at Stockholm University, where he was a researcher at SciLifeLab.
His laboratory studied the energy-conversion processes of mitochondria, focusing on the mechanisms, assembly and evolution of bioenergetic complexes.
In addition, Amunts helped establish the Swedish National Cryo-Electron Microscopy Facility. In 2023, he received the Westlake Senior Research Fellow Award, which supported research leave in Hangzhou in eastern China’s Zhejiang province.
“My sabbatical at Westlake University in Hangzhou, hosted by Yigong Shi, made one thing very clear to me: China is undergoing a genuine shift in its scientific model. The emphasis is moving towards originality and building new capabilities rather than simply refining what already exists,” Amunts said.


In 2024, he joined the Max Planck Institute of Molecular Physiology in Dortmund as a Humboldt Scholar, a lifetime honour conferred by Germany’s Alexander von Humboldt Foundation to recognise outstanding international researchers. The foundation, established in 1860, is named after the German geographer and naturalist Alexander von Humboldt.
After leaving Dortmund, he returned to China, choosing Shenzhen as the next stop in his academic career.
The day after the announcement, SMART posted that Amunts’ research team was recruiting for positions ranging from associate researcher and postdoctoral fellow to lab manager.
The recruitment notice states that the team will use world-class research platforms, foster an active open international environment and offer competitive salaries and benefits.

data.title: UBS conveys upbeat note on Chinese stocks in 2026 as global investors seek diversification
data.description:
UBS Group conveyed a sanguine outlook on Chinese stocks in 2026, as the world’s second-largest economy proved to be an important alternative for global investors seeking to diversify away from US equities, which face headwinds from stretched valuations to jitters about the independence of the Federal Reserve.
Chinese stocks are expected to be energised by the nation’s growing innovation capability, greater adoption of artificial intelligence across traditional industries and potential inflows from mainland household savings and global investors, according to senior UBS executives at Tuesday’s opening of the Swiss bank’s two-day annual China conference in Shanghai.
Corporate earnings might contribute more to equity gains this year, rather than valuation expansion, as profit growth of 10 per cent was expected, they said.
“Chinese assets’ appeal will further increase this year because of a sharpened focus on diversified investments,” said Thomas Fang, head of China global markets at UBS. “China is expected to become an important market for multi-allocations by global capital. China stocks will have a bumper year on innovation capability, ample liquidity policy support and potential inflows.”
That assessment underscored how investors’ preference for Chinese stocks over US equity had extended into 2026, as Beijing’s push for tech self-reliance is projected to boost the weightings of emerging strategic industries on the mainland.
Chinese stocks beat US equities last year, as US exceptionalism that powered years of strong equity performance abated, following initial signs of an AI bubble emerging and the Fed’s latest interest rate cuts that encouraged increased investment in Asia-based shares.
In 2026, the CSI 300 Index has so far risen about 3 per cent, outpacing a 1.9 per cent gain in the S&P 500, which substantiated the trend towards diversification. The Chinese benchmark jumped 18 per cent last year, while the US gauge rose 16 per cent.

The revaluation of Chinese stocks was still under way, according to Fang, as UBS engaged with more overseas clients over the past year.
James Wang, a UBS strategist, said there was more room for global fund managers to boost their exposure, as Chinese stocks still had a valuation edge over overseas equities.
AI would be a key theme to trade on Chinese stocks throughout the year, according to Wang, as AI-related companies in China had a low correlation with their US peers, which were vulnerable to negative news headlines owing to their frothy valuations.
Wang said he preferred Chinese semiconductor equipment makers, AI hardware vendors, brokerages, solar firms and other companies with go-global strategies.
Signs that investors were diversifying from US stocks had emerged heading into 2026, as the administration of US President Donald Trump escalated its efforts to gain control of the Fed.
In a Sunday night statement, Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell said the Department of Justice served the agency “with grand jury subpoenas, threatening a criminal indictment related to my testimony before the Senate Banking Committee ⁠last June”.
Powell called that action a “pretext” by the Trump administration to gain more influence over the central bank and US monetary policy.
For investors, that move appeared to threaten the credibility of monetary policy setting and spur a capital flight to safe-haven investments such as gold and silver.
Meanwhile, the combined daily turnovers on the Shanghai and Shenzhen exchanges hit a record 3.58 trillion yuan (US$513.2 billion) on Monday, with the CSI 300 hovering around a four-year high.
According to UBS strategist Meng Lei, that spike in trading values should not be interpreted as a sign of overheating, as openings of new stock accounts rose at a moderate pace in 2025 and the proportion of margin trading as a percentage of the capitalisation of key stock gauges remained low.

data.title: Meet Bobbi Salvör Menuez, who plays Ainsley’s roommate, Landman’s first non-binary character
data.description:
The penultimate episode of the hit neo-Western series Landman aired last weekend on Paramount+, setting the show up for an exciting finale. Created by Taylor Sheridan and Christian Wallace, the show, based on the famous Boomtown podcast, is currently one of the most popular TV titles on the platform – second only to South Park, according to streaming data collection website FlixPatrol.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Landman (@landmanpplus)



Despite episode nine being the second-last of the season, Landman isn’t done introducing new characters. As Ainsley Norris (Michelle Randolph) – Tommy (Billy Bob Thornton) and Angela (Ali Larter) Norris’ daughter – starts her freshman year at Texas Christian University, she meets her new roommate, Paigyn Meester.












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by Landman (@landmanpplus)



Meester, played by Bobbi Salvör Menuez, goes against everything Ainsley thought she knew about the world. They are a non-binary, vegan ferret owner – and Norris does not get along well with them.
So, what else do we know about Bobbi Salvör Menuez?
What’s their background?












View this post on Instagram























A post shared by bobbi salvör menuez (@hi_its_bobbi)



Menuez was born in May 1993. In 2019, they announced on Instagram that they would be going by Bobbi Salvör Menuez. Menuez is trans and non-binary and uses they/them pronouns.
They were raised in Park Slope, Brooklyn, and lived briefly in London until their parents separated, they told LGBTQ entertainment website Autostraddle. Their mother worked in retail, and their father made furniture, but both were also creatives, per Vanity Fair.
Menuez grew up with gay aunts and uncles – close friends rather than biological relatives. One of their close uncles lived with Aids, they told the NYC Tourism website as part of its “Trans Voices in NYC” series. In high school, Menuez co-founded an art collective called Luck You, per i-D magazine.
Where have you seen them before?

Menuez has a wide range of films and TV shows under their belt. You may have seen them in films like Nocturnal Animals (2016), Adam (2019) and My Animal (2023). They were a writer on the 2024 film Lunacy and worked as a costume designer on 2011’s Kuichisan. They are also known for appearances in TV series like Euphoria and I Love Dick.
In addition, they’re a model, an artist, the editor-at-large of Purple Fashion Magazine, and co-organiser of Mother Daughter Holy Spirit, through which they raise funds for the Trans Justice Funding Project.
They’re married

Menuez is married to artist Quori Theodor, per Vogue. The pair met through mutual friends and proposed to each other before getting married in 2024. Menuez donned a Miss Claire Sullivan piece for the ceremony, while Theodor opted for Zoe Gustavia Anna Whalen.
The couple are also collaborators and run a queer cooking collective called Spiral Theory Test Kitchen together, alongside fellow artist Precious Okoyomon.

data.title: Chinese social media users hit out at Fan Zhendong’s Saarbrücken teammates after defeat
data.description:
Chinese social media users have hit out at the performances of Fan Zhendong’s teammates following 1. FC Saarbrücken TT’s loss to Borussia Dortmund in the Bundesliga on Monday.
Reigning Olympic table tennis champion Fan won both of his matches – against Alberto Mino and Kristian Karlsson – 3-0.
However, Fan’s dominance was not enough to stop his German club team from losing 3-2.
The team’s fourth defeat of the season means the newly crowned German Cup champions slipped down the table to third place out of 12 teams. Only the top four teams qualify for the play-offs.
Fan’s performance was in stark contrast to team captain Patrick Franziska from Germany, who lost both his singles and doubles match.
Franziska’s doubles partner Eduard Ionescu from Romania also lost his singles match.
Despite the defeat, Saarbrücken heaped praise on Fan.
“Fan Zhendong’s performance was dominant, but unfortunately, we narrowly lost the decisive doubles match, suffering a setback in the play-off race,” the club said on social media.

“Now we’re fully focused on the coming battles with Post SV Mühlhausen and GV Hennebont TT.”
Former world No 1 Fan, who withdrew from the International Table Tennis Federation rankings in December 2024, beat Ecuadorian Mino 11-4, 11-9, 11-5.
Leading by a game but trailing 9-4 in his second match against Karlsson, Fan reeled off seven consecutive points to steal the game. He then went on to win the match 3-0.
After the defeat, Chinese social media users were quick to praise the decorated player from Guangzhou, but there were more comments blaming his teammates.
Team captain Franziska was the focus of some comments, with users suggesting he “didn’t do enough”.
“If the team continues to play like this, it’s not surprising that Saarbrücken won’t win the championship,” one Weibo user said.
“Fan Zhendong was unbeatable in the singles; unfortunately, his teammates couldn’t keep up with the pace,” another commentator added.
“If one player can play in three matches, surely Fan would wish he could go up and fight again,” another Weibo commentator said.
Saarbrücken now face a busy schedule on the road, with an away match in the league against Mühlhausen on Saturday, followed by a trip to France to take on Hennebont in the first leg of the Champions League quarter-final a day later.

data.title: Malaysian petrol station operators fume as foreign fuel abusers go unpunished
data.description:
Two cases within a week involving foreign-registered cars receiving subsidised RON95 fuel have led to calls for Malaysia’s government to go after the vehicle owners as well rather than just the petrol station operators.
The common view was that it was unfair for the operators to be the only party penalised while the vehicle owners were let off the hook.
“Since there are no penalties, these culprits will try their luck,” said a cashier at a petrol station, who only wanted to be known as Lee.
He said they were doing their best to prevent such attempts from drivers of foreign-registered vehicles.
“So far, I have not encountered such cases but I know of previous colleagues who had seen a Singaporean car owner trying to use his credit card to buy RON95 petrol,” Lee said.
On January 3, a man was captured on video pumping subsidised RON95 fuel into a Singapore-registered car with its registration number partially covered with black tape.
When the clip went viral, the car owner, a permanent resident of Singapore, was reported to have surrendered at the Kulai district police headquarters in Johor to assist with investigations.
Last Saturday, CCTV footage of a petrol station in Sungai Petani, Kedah, showed subsidised RON95 petrol being pumped into a foreign-registered vehicle.
The Domestic Trade and Cost of Living Ministry in Kedah is investigating the case.
A petrol station attendant in Johor Bahru, Satria Sulaiman, said they had been keeping a close watch on all vehicles, especially those with Singapore-registered plates.
“However, some may still slip through. We have been reminded often and are well aware of the consequences of failing to be vigilant,” Satria said.
“We pay close attention to each vehicle coming in but during peak hours, there is a possibility that we may overlook.”
“It does not help that there are no penalties for those committing or attempting to cheat,” Satria, 33, said in an interview.
Lawyer Norman Fernandez said the government should urgently enact a law to complement the Control of Supplies Act to ensure that offending vehicle owners are equally liable.
This story was first published by The Star

data.title: Beijing urges Hong Kong to take lead in globalising traditional Chinese medicine
data.description:
China’s top health official has urged Hong Kong to serve as a key pillar in promoting traditional Chinese medicine globally, as the city’s leader pledged to align local policies with the national development strategy in the field.
At a launch ceremony for the Chinese Medicine Hospital and nearby testing institute on Tuesday, National Health Commission head Lei Haichao offered three pieces of advice for Hong Kong in advancing the development of traditional Chinese Medicine.
“Serve the globe for the benefit of humanity and accelerate the internationalisation of Chinese Medicine,” he said.
“Hong Kong should leverage its technical strength in Chinese medicine testing and quality control, turning it into a key support pillar for the global expansion of Chinese medicine.”
Lei also called on the Chinese Medicine Hospital – which opened last month – to develop an integrated Chinese-Western treatment model and disease-specific programmes tailored to local needs.
He added that the hospital should collaborate with the Hong Kong Science Park and the Hetao Shenzhen–Hong Kong Science and Technology Innovation Cooperation Zone on clinical research and technology transfer.

The hospital also plays a vital role in positioning Hong Kong as a top talent hub for Chinese medicine and in deepening cooperation within the Greater Bay Area, Lei said.
“The opening of the hospital is a catalyst to establish a Chinese medicine medical consortium and cluster in the Greater Bay Area… it should also provide workstations for Chinese medicine masters to attract experts from all over the world,” he said.
Speaking at the same event, Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu said he aimed to turn Hong Kong into a “bridgehead” for traditional Chinese medicine to go global.
“The Hong Kong government actively aligns with national strategies, fully integrating into and serving the overall development of traditional Chinese medicine in the country,” Lee said.
“Chinese medicine is an integral part of Hong Kong’s healthcare system, and our medical system, regulatory framework, standard-setting and clinical research are all aligned with international standards.”
The Chinese Medicine Hospital and the permanent premises of the Government Chinese Medicine Testing Institute – located side by side in Tseung Kwan O – officially opened on December 11.

The hospital serves not only as a healthcare provider but also as a training and research hub.
Lee said it has been popular with the public and had increased its service quota three times within its first month of operation due to high demand.
The testing institute, first established in 2017 and moved to its permanent building last month, is dedicated to setting internationally recognised reference standards for Chinese medicines and testing methods.
Lee said the two flagship institutes have established a closely intertwined Chinese medicine ecosystem – linking institutions and hospital services, research and industry.

data.title: Iranians use Musk’s Starlink to pierce internet blackout during protests
data.description:
Some Iranians were still using Elon Musk’s Starlink satellite internet service despite a nationwide communications blackout, three people inside the ‍country said, the latest example of Starlink being used to counter internet shutdowns in geopolitical flashpoints.
Iranian authorities have in recent days launched a deadly crackdown on nationwide protests, including the near-complete shutdown of internet service, which is provided through fibre-optic cables and mobile phone towers.
The blackout has made it difficult to track the full scope of the protest movement. Iran’s Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi on Monday said access should be restored “very soon”.
US-based rights group HRANA said by late Monday it had verified the deaths of 646 people, including 505 protesters, 113 military and security personnel and seven bystanders, and was investigating 579 more reported deaths.

Since the protests began on December 28, 10,721 people have been arrested, the group said.
Iranian authorities have blamed the unrest ‍on terrorists and vowed to safeguard the governing system.
Starlink, which beams its service directly from thousands of low-Earth orbit satellites, was still working in some places in Iran, despite being ⁠banned by authorities there, three people using Starlink in the country told Reuters.
One of them, in Western Iran, said he knew dozens of people using Starlink and that users in border towns and cities were largely unaffected.
Alp Toker, founder of internet monitoring group NetBlocks, said he has heard from people in the region that there was still some Starlink access in Iran, though service appeared reduced.
“It is patchy, but still there,” he said. Toker added a broader internet blackout that began in ‍Iran on January 8 continued on Monday, with non-satellite connectivity at around 1 per cent of usual levels in the country, based on the fixed-line and mobile internet data NetBlocks tracks.

On Tuesday, some mobile phones in Iran were able to call abroad. Several people in Tehran were able to call Associated Press and speak to a journalist there. The AP bureau in Dubai, United Arab Emirates, was unable to call those numbers back.
Iranians said text messaging appeared to remain down, and witnesses said the internet remained cut off from the outside world.
While ‍it was not clear how Starlink’s service was being disrupted in Iran, some specialists said it could be the result of jamming of Starlink terminals that would overpower their ability to receive signals from the satellites.
Starlink, which is part of the privately held US company SpaceX, did not respond to requests for comment.
The communication lifeline Starlink has provided for some protesters in Iran was the latest sign of the influence Musk and his satellite internet service have in global conflicts and unrest around the world.
Starlink has been a critical tool for Ukrainian forces since Russia’s full-scale invasion in 2022.

In Myanmar, where the military junta has imposed repeated internet shutdowns, ‌rebel groups, aid ⁠organisations and medics have used Starlink for communications.
In Sudan, Starlink has also been used by both sides in a years-long civil war due to prolonged blackouts.
US President Donald Trump said on Sunday he planned to speak with Musk about restoring internet in Iran, without mentioning Starlink. Musk previously shipped free Starlink terminals, which are slightly larger than a laptop, to Ukraine and offered free internet service there.
Standard Starlink terminals cost around US$599, plus a monthly service fee, making it unaffordable for many ‌Iranians.
Musk’s ability to tip the balance in global conflicts was demonstrated when he shut down Starlink as Ukraine retook territory from Russia in 2022, Reuters previously reported.
Starlink is not licensed to operate in Iran, but Musk has previously said the service was active there. In December 2022, he ‌posted on his social media platform X ‌that the company was “approaching 100 Starlinks active in Iran”, a modest figure given Iran’s population of 92 million.
In June last year, in response to a post on X calling for him to provide Starlink access to Iran, Musk posted “beams are on”.
Following the 12-day war between Iran and Israel in June, Iran’s parliament passed a law formally banning the use of Starlink, introducing severe penalties for those who use or distribute the unlicensed technology, according to Iranian state media.
Additional reporting by Associated Press and Bloomberg
